Distribution: Andaman Isls, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Borneo, Cambodia, China, India, Java, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, New Guinea, Philippines, Sri Lanka, Thailand, Vietnam. PANAY. Native.

(L.) L.Rico , Anales Jard. Bot. Madrid 63 (2006) 210; ≡ Acacia glauca (L.) Moench , Methodus (Moench) (1794) 466 ; Faridah Hanum & Van der Maesen, PROSEA 11 (1997) 58; ≡ Mimosa glauca L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 520. = Acaciella villosa (Sw.) Britton & Rose , N. Amer. Fl. 23 (1928) 104; ≡ Mimosa villosa Sw. , Prodr. (Swartz) (1788) 85.
species.distribution: America.
species.notes: Naturalized (PNH 7014)? P.B. Pelser (11-Feb-22): Rico A. & Bachman (2006) explain that Acaciella glauca (L.) L.Rico and Acaciella villosa (Sw.) Britton & Rose are taxonomically distinct, but it is unclear to me which of these was introduced to the Philippines.

species.distribution: India to China, Japan, Malay Peninsula, Philippines, Vietnam. BABUYAN ISLS, BALABAC, BASILAN, BOHOL, LUZON: Mountain Province, NCR, Pangasinan, Rizal, MINDANAO: Agusan del Norte, Misamis Oriental (photos), PALAWAN, POLILLO, SIASI.
species.notes: N Luzon to Babuyan Isls to Mindanao and Palawan. Open grasslands and thickets. Some forms cultivated. 1200m (photos).

species.citation: (Willd.) Ohwi & H.Ohashi , J. Jap. Bot. 44 (1969) 29; ≡ Dolichos angularis Willd. , Sp. Pl., 4th Ed. 3 (1802) 1051.
species.distribution: C America, China, India, ?Philippines, Vietnam.

species.citation: (L.) Walp. , Repert. Bot. Syst. (Walpers) 1 (1842) 799; ≡ Dolichos unguiculatus L. , Sp. Pl., 1st Ed. (1753) 725. = Vigna sinensis (L.) Savi ex Hassk. , Cat. Hort. Bot. Bogor. (Hasskarl) (1844) 279 ; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 320; ≡ Dolichos sinensis L. , Cent. Pl. II (1756) 28. ssp. sesquipedalis (L.) Verdc., Fl. Turkey 3 (1970) 266; Kew Bull. 24 (1970) 544; Pancho & Gruezo, Vasc. Fl. Mt Makiling 2 (2006) 114; ≡ Dolichos sesquipedalis L., Sp. Pl., 2nd Ed. (1763) 1019. Notes: Probably a native of China. Cultivated pantropically. Widely cultivated in the Philippines for its edible pods and beans. Yardlong bean; sitaw. Cultivated, not naturalized.
species.notes: Cultivated in all tropical and warm-temperate countries. Widely grown in the Philippines. Cowpea.

species.citation: Koord. , Meded. Lands Plantentuin 19 (1898) 446 ; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 245; Nielsen, Fl. Males. ser. 1, 11 (1992) 164. = Pithecellobium williamsii Elmer , LPB 1 (1907) 223; Type: Elmer 8833 (L, NY, iso), Luzon: Benguet, Baguio, on a ridge of the Sablan canyon, Mar-1907.
species.distribution: Philippines, Sulawesi. BORACAY (photos), BURIAS, CALAYAN, CAMIGUIN DE BABUYANES, CEBU, LUZON: Aurora, Benguet, Cagayan, Camarines, Camarines Sur, Isabela, Laguna, NCR, Quezon, MASBATE, MINDORO, NEGROS, SAMAR, TICAO.
species.notes: Lowland forests, especially near the seashore, but also inland to a considerable elevation. 0-850m.

species.citation: Elmer , LPB 2 (1910) 743 ; Dunn, Kew Bull. (1912) 364; Merrill, EPFP 2 (1923) 280; ≡ Callerya scandens (Elmer) Schot , Blumea 39 (1994) 31; ≡ Whitfordia scandens Elmer , LPB 2 (1910) 689, nom. illeg.; Type: Elmer 12259.
species.distribution: Endemic to the Philippines. CEBU, MINDANAO: Agusan del Norte, Bukidnon, NEGROS, PALAWAN, PANAY, SIBUYAN.
species.notes: Whitfordia is a nom. illeg. because it is a genus of fungi. Low and medium elevation forests.

species.citation: Span. , Linnaea 15 (1841) 192 ; Mohlenbrock, Webbia 16 (1962) 652. = Zornia cantoniensis Mohlenbr. , Webbia 16 (1961) 124 ; Webbia 16 (1962) 653. = Lupinus angustifolius Blanco , Fl. Filip., 1st Ed. (1837) 566. = Smithia bigeminata Blanco , Fl. Filip., 2nd Ed. (1845) 395 ; Fl. Filip. 3, 2 (1878) 362. ≠ Zornia diphylla auct. non (L.) Pers. ; Merrill, Fl. Manila (1912) 238; EPFP 2 (1923) 283; Meeuwen et al., Reinwardtia 5 (1960) 453; ?Moody, Weeds reported in rice in South and Southeast Asia (1989) 297.
species.distribution: Andaman Isls, Cambodia, China, India, Indian Ocean, Japan, Java, Lesser Sunda Isls, Malay Peninsula, Myanmar, Nepal, New Guinea, Pakistan, Philippines, Ryukyu Isls, Sri Lanka, Sulawesi, Sumatra, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam. LUZON: Bataan, Benguet, Cagayan, Ilocos, Ilocos Norte, NCR, Quezon, Rizal, Tarlac, Zambales.
species.notes: An example of a class of plants which, though not absent under everwet climatic conditions favors those areas which are subject to a dry period. In N and C Luzon; in open dry grasslands in the settled areas at low and medium elevation, with all the appearance of an introduced species.
